text: hi this is dr george i'm going to be doing a little bit of a voiceover on a gastric bypass that we did robotically recently for those of you that are learning about gastric bypasses this is a weight loss surgery that we perform for people with chronic weight problems and it's in a very very effective operation we do it minimally invasively with
five little tiny incisions.
And on average, it takes us anywhere between about an hour to an hour and a half, depending on technical factors.
Here, the patient is already asleep under general anesthesia.
We've pumped some carbon dioxide gas into the belly to see what we're doing.
And we have a robotic surgical platform that we're using to perform the operation.
What you see me doing here is basically we've identified the small intestine and we're putting some marking stitches into kind of
help us with the orientation of the small intestine as far as which part is proximal, which part is distal.
And once we've finished placing our marking stitches on the small intestine, now we're going to come up here and take a look at her stomach.

And I usually do a preoperative endoscopy, which is a small camera down the mouth on patients before surgery, just to take a look at the stomach on the inside and to see if they have something called a hiatal hernia.
And then when I do the surgery,
As in this case, I take a look at the diaphragm on the outside just to see if that hiatal hernia is there.
And hiatal hernia is basically where it's a little loose when the esophagus comes down through the diaphragm.
I don't see one really standing out on this particular patient.
So we're going to go ahead and start doing our dissection to create our gastric pouch.
And so this is the proximal stomach that we've identified.
That's called the angle of hiss.
We're basically kind of taking down some of the attachments up there.
And that's going to be our landing spot for where we're going to come up with our staple load that you'll see here in a couple of minutes.
And once we have that dissected out, now I'm basically dissecting out the area along the lesser curvature of the stomach.
And we're going to kind of get into the space behind the stomach.
so that we have basically a pathway to staple.
Now you can see here that we've introduced a robotic stapler into the abdomen after we have gotten our dissection done with the back of the stomach there.
And after making sure that everything is lined up, we start our stapling process.
And this basically fires several rows of permanent staples on each side and then cuts in between that.
And so what it's doing is it's basically starting to form the beginning of our gastric pouch.
And unlike with a sleeve gastrectomy, we don't remove any stomach from the patient's body.
So we are making a very small stomach pouch here, but the stomach is divided.
And so the small stomach pouch that you'll see here, eventually once it's created,
is where the food's going to come down after you swallow.
The rest of the stomach that's divided is still in the body, but it's never going to see food again.

Now what I'm doing here is I'm basically taking a robotic instrument and I'm putting it up to that last part of the space behind the stomach towards that initial dissection where we started off doing the dissection around the angle of hiss.
And that gives me a pretty good sort of landing spot for the last couple of staple loads that we're going to use.
So after this last firing of the stapler, you're going to see that the small stomach pouch is on the left-hand side and the remainder of the stomach or the remnant stomach is on the right-hand side.
So we're basically giving patients about 5% to 10% of their stomach at the end that's going to see food.
So it's really kind of significantly diminishing the capacity for food
And here now we're making a small hole in the stomach pouch.
We're going to do the same thing on the small bowel side.
And we're preparing to create a connection between the small intestine and the stomach up there.
Now, once we have our surgical created holes in the small intestine and the stomach, we're essentially going to use a robotic stapler again to help create the connection.
And as I mentioned before, the stapler is firing several rows of staples on each side and cutting in between them.
So what this is doing is it's basically helping to create a common
connection between the stomach and the small intestine here.
And now we're going to be closing up this defect that we have here from where we created the surgical openings in the small intestine and the stomach.
And so we're going to use a special kind of suture here to close this in different layers.
Well, I've done this gastric bypass using a method called an omega loop, which means that we've brought a loop of small intestine up.
And initially I put marking sutures on the small intestine to kind of help us orientate proximal and distal small bowel.
And I'm just kind of double checking that orientation now before we make a window in the mesentery here to divide the small bowel.

now what we're going to do is we're going to basically run what's called our roux limb and this is where the name roux and why gastric bypass comes from this is essentially the length of small intestine down which food after it's been swallowed and after it's passed through the small stomach pouch is going to travel before it mixes with the digestive juices and enzymes and so you know i typically

we'll run this rule in around 150 centimeters or so, uh, depending on, you know, it's, it depends on the patient's starting weight and BMI and, and, uh, but, uh, in this pressure, in this patient's case, uh, we did about 150 centimeter rule.
And now we're going to do our second connection here.

which is basically helping to create a connection between what's called the biliopancreatic limb or the small intestine up there, which drains all the digestive juices from the liver, the pancreas, and gallbladder, if it's still there, into what's called the common channel or the alimentary limb, which is where the food is going to be coming down from the stomach pouch.

Now, once we have completed this second anastomosis or connection between the biliopancreatic limb and the elementary limb, we are going to close what's called the mesenteric defect here, which is basically a potential space where you can get an internal hernia.
And if you think about your intestines and your mesentery sort of like a curtain rod and a curtain, when we divide the small bowels we did earlier,
you are also sort of creating a gap here in the mesentery or the curtain.

So we're kind of closing this defect here and the idea is to basically try to reduce the risk of having an internal hernia, which is where a piece of small bowel can come in through here and basically twist on itself and cause problems.
So for people that have a gastric bypass, usually the greatest risk of having an internal hernia is
around up to about two to three years after their surgery when you've had a lot of their weight loss happen.
Because when you lose weight and melt fat away on the outside, you're also melting fat on the inside.
And sometimes you can have these gaps that open up in the mesentery and create these spaces and these defects where you can get a hernia.

So I always tell my patients after gastric bypass that, you know, if you develop any type of unexplained abdominal pain, constipation, nausea, vomiting, something just doesn't feel right with your belly,
you need to go directly to the hospital or notify your surgeon, go to ER and let them know you had a gastric bypass and make sure you get a surgical evaluation.
So, you know, the good news is that oftentimes we can fix these internal hernias, you know, once again, with a small camera and the small incisions.
Also, the risk is pretty low.
It's about one to 3% out to three years postoperatively.
And then that risk
decreases.
So it never goes higher than that.
It decreases after you have your weight loss, but you still are going to have a risk for an internal hernia the rest of your life.
It's very, very low, but I've seen internal hernias happen 20 years after gastric bypass.
So even though it's a rare occurrence, it is something to think about if you get a gastric bypass.
